metadata.title: The AI Editor That Actually Made My Writing Better
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.

metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. There's a kindness problem in writing education: teachers stay too gentle to say what is actually wrong. Without honest feedback, you can't see or close the gap in your own writing. Eddy, built by Prof. Nik Bear Brown, gives blunt, usable editing instead of gentle praise. After using it, the writer finally had an actual, repeatable revision process.

artifactLines: There's a kindness problem in writing education: teachers stay too gentle to say what is actually wrong.
Without honest feedback, you can't see or close the gap in your own writing.
Eddy, built by Prof. Nik Bear Brown, gives blunt, usable editing instead of gentle praise.
After using it, the writer finally had an actual, repeatable revision process.

beats.narration_text: I related to the 'kindness problem' you named, teachers being too gentle to tell you what's actually wrong, and how Eddy finally gave you a real process. I want to build one too. Help me: (1) explain what Eddy does that human kindness tends to avoid, (2) turn that into a repeatable revision loop I could run on my own draft, and (3) name a specific weakness a blunt editor catches that a gentle one misses.

output: I used to think editing meant running Grammarly and fixing the re
Eddy applies that same philosophy to writing

beats.narration_text: Eddy was built by Prof. I didn’t know how to close it until I tried a tool called Eddy the Editor.
